The preexisting tests did not detect this because they check for array equality, where `Any[] == Char[]` is satisfied.

### Before

```julia-repl
julia> x = readeach(IOBuffer(), Char);

julia> eltype(x)
Any

julia> collect(x)
Any[]
```

### After

```julia-repl
julia> x = readeach(IOBuffer(), Char);

julia> eltype(x)
Char

julia> collect(x)
Char[]

speculative"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2021-04-06T13:19:24Z","updated_at":"2024-11-25T15:19:09Z","closed_at":"2024-11-25T15:19:09Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"These are a couple of ideas to extend those proposed in #22891.

- As a natural extension to `break`-with-value in a loop, I propose `continue` to append a value to a returned array.

- I had initially thought `default` would be more descriptive than `else` and may lessen objections to it (https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/23260#issuecomment-463768852). However, @tkluck pointed out (https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/23260#issuecomment-813968476) that this would not be backwards compatible because 'default' is not a keyword. What about `finally`? It makes sense in context with loops; after iteration it is the final block of code to run.

- The `return` keyword could be used to explicitly exit the `finally` block.

- Are there any issues with using a type declaration on the assigned variable that would need to be addressed as well?

```Julia
values = rand(1:100, 10)

evens::Array{Int64} = for i in values # optional return type
    if iseven(i)
        continue i # append to returned array
    elseif i == 13
        continue "not Int64" # throw an exception
    end
finally # or `else`
    newvalues = values * 2
    return newvalues # explicit return
end
```

P.S. This is my first foray into github, I welcome constructive criticism/feedback :)

PR creates a simple `Base.Fix` struct as a general version of `Base.Fix1` and `Base.Fix2` for creating type-stable closures.

Along with the PR I also add a larger suite of unittests for all three of these functions to complement the existing tests for `Fix1`/`Fix2`.

### Context

There are multiple motivations for this generalization:

1. To generalize `Fix1` and `Fix2`, and potentially be used as a more general and unified replacement for their functionality.
2. From a language design perspective, I would argue that `Fix` is nice in a few different ways, including:
    - Principle of Least Astonishment – you would expect `Fix1`, `Fix2`, etc., to continue in a sequence. I actually expected this when first learning Julia, which is why I had first raised [#50553](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50553).
    - This is similar to how `literal_pow` has a fallback general method for any `Val{N}`, but explicit methods for `Val{1}` and `Val{2}`. `Fix` is just the general form of `Fix(1,2)`.
3. To help provide a simple way to produce type-stable closures. Type stability issues can occur when a closure captures a variable: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/15276, and so `Fix1`, `Fix2`, and now `Fix` are nice shorthands for capturing with stability.
4. If you have a lot of arguments, and only need to fix one of them, this is a nice shorthand for what would be a very long closure.
5. To allow for more complex functional programming than possible with `Fix1` and `Fix2` which limit one to 2-argument functions.
    - I ran into this when starting out in Julia, and even now sometimes forget this until the error message shows up due to me forgetting the 2-argument constraint. `Fix` allows partial functions with any number of arguments, both before and after the insertion, as well as the insertion of an arbitrary number of arguments. In this way it attempts to reduce cognitive load when creating type-stable closures.

The function `Fix` is pretty simple as you can see in the code below so I think is a worthy addition.

### Details

Using `Base.Fix1` and `Base.Fix2` are very useful ways to get around some of the potential issues of closures. However, as the names suggest, they can only inject arguments at the first and second index. Furthermore, they are also constrained to work on 2-argument functions exclusively, (perhaps as a vestige of some earlier Julia designs which are now no longer necessary). It seems at various points there had been interest in extending this (see links below) but nobody had gotten around to it so far, so I thought I would take a stab at it as a short project!

This implementation of `Base.Fix` generalises the form as follows:

>`Fix(f, n, x...)`
>`Fix(f, Val(n), x...)`
>
> A type representing a partially-applied version of a function `f`, with the argument or arguments "x" inserted at the `n`th position. In other words, `Fix(f, Val(n), x1, x2)` behaves similarly to `(y...,) -> f(y[1:N-1]..., x1, x2, y[N:end]...)`
> 
> You may also pass a number of arguments to `Fix`, with `Val(n)`, in which case they will be inserted at position `n`, `n+1`, and so forth.

This means that `Fix` will work on functions with arbitrary number of arguments, and splat the fixed arguments into the `N`th position. 

For example, you can reproduce functionality similar to `Fix1` and `Fix2`:

```julia
Fix1(f, x) = Base.Fix(f, 1, x)
Fix2(f, x) = Base.Fix(f, 2, x)
```

Although note that `Fix` allows for a `Vararg` *after* the inserted argument as well.

In other words, `Fix` it lets you specify any number of arguments, rather than just 2-argument functions. The code which is executed is:

```julia
function (f::Fix{F,N,T})(args::Vararg{Any,M}) where {F,N,T,M}
    @inline
    M < N - 1 && N != 1 && throw(ArgumentError("expected at least $(N-1) arguments to a `Fix` function with `N=$(N)`"))
    return f.f(args[begin:begin+(N-2)]..., f.x..., args[begin+(N-1):end]...)
end
```

This means that the captured variable `x` is inserted at the `N`-th position.

I have added several unittests of this, a docstring, as well as type stability checks, for which it seems to succeed.

I also run the new `Fix1` and `Fix2` test suites added by this PR on the `Fix` version of each struct.

### Examples

**Simple example:**

To fix the argument `f` with an anonymous function:

```julia
with_f = (a, b, c, d, e, g, h, i, j, k) -> my_func(a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h, i, j, k)
```

whereas now it becomes:

```julia
with_f = Base.Fix(my_func, 6, f)
```


**<details><summary>Closure stability:</summary>**

Say that you have a function with a type instability due to a captured variable in a closure:

```julia
function f(x, y)
    function g()
        x = x^2
        y = y * 3.5
        x + y
    end
    g()
end
```

Due to the behavior reported in #15276, this is unstable, and Julia infers it to be `Any`.

`Fix` can help address this issue as follows:

```julia
function f(x, y)
    g = Fix(1, x, y) do x, y
        x = x^2
        y = y * 3.5
        x + y
    end
    g()
end
```

which is now type stable. `Fix` lets you be a bit more flexible here, as you can do this for multiple arguments.

</details>

**<details><summary>In a reduction</summary>**

`Fix1` and `Fix2` are useful for short lambda functions like

```julia
sum(Base.Fix1(*, 2), [1, 2, 3, 4, 5])
```

to reduce compilation and often improve type stability.

With `Fix`, you can aren't limited to only 2-arg functions, so you can use things like `fma` in this context:

```julia
sum(Base.Fix(fma, 2, 0.5, 2.0), [1, 2, 3, 4, 5])
```

where `Fix{typeof(affine),2,Tuple{Float64,Float64}}` will evaluate as `x -> affine(x, 0.5, 2.0)`.

Another example is a `mapreduce`, where you would typically want to fix the map and reduction in applying:

```julia
sum(
    Base.Fix(mapreduce, 1, abs, *),
    [[1, -1], [2, -3, 4], [5]]
)
```

</details>

**<details><summary>In data processing pipelines**</summary>

`Fix` can be used to set any number of arguments, and of course also be chained together repeatedly as there is no restriction on 2-arg functions.  It makes functional programming easier than with only `Fix1` and `Fix2`.

For example, in a processing pipeline:

using CSV, DataFrames

```julia
affine(a, b, c) = a .+ b .* c

affine_transform_df(path) = (
    CSV.read(path, DataFrame)
        |> dropmissing
        |> Fix(     filter, 1, :id => ==(7)) # Use like Fix2
        |> Fix(     affine, 2, 0.5, 2.0)    # Multiple args
        |> Fix(getproperty, 2, :a)
        |> Fix(  mapreduce, 1, abs, *)      # mapreduce has input as 3rd argument
)

affine_transform_df("data.csv")
```

</details>

### Features in other languages

Here are some of the most related features in other languages (all that I could find; there's probably more)

#### Groovy's `.ncurry`

<details><summary>(Expand)</summary>

In Apache Groovy there is the `<function>.ncurry(index, args...)` to insert any number of arguments at a given index.  This syntax is semantically identical to `Base.Fix`.

From the [documentation](https://web.archive.org/web/20240522202230/https://groovy-lang.org/closures.html#_index_based_currying)
> In case a closure accepts more than 2 parameters, it is possible to set an arbitrary parameter using ncurry:
>
> ```groovy
> def volume = { double l, double w, double h -> l*w*h }
> def fixedWidthVolume = volume.ncurry(1, 2d)
> assert volume(3d, 2d, 4d) == fixedWidthVolume(3d, 4d)
> def fixedWidthAndHeight = volume.ncurry(1, 2d, 4d)
> assert volume(3d, 2d, 4d) == fixedWidthAndHeight(3d)
> ```
>
> 1. the `volume` function defines 3 parameters
> 2. `ncurry` will set the second parameter (index = 1) to 2d, creating a new volume function which accepts length and height
> 3. that function is equivalent to calling `volume` omitting the width
> 4. it is also possible to set multiple parameters, starting from the specified index
> 5. the resulting function accepts as many parameters as the initial one minus the number of parameters set by `ncurry`

</details>

#### Python's `functools.partial`

<details><summary>(Expand)</summary>

In Python, there is no differentiating between args and kwargs – every function can be passed kwargs.  Therefore, `functools.partial` is semantically similar to `Fix`:

```python
def f(a, b, c, d):
    return a + b * c - d

f_with_b = functools.partial(f, b=2.0, c=3.0)

f_with_b(1.0, d=3.0)
```

which would be equivalent to `Fix(f, 2, 2.0, 3.0)`.

</details>

#### C++'s `std::bind`

<details><summary>(Expand)</summary>

In modern C++, one can use `std::bind` to insert [placeholders](https://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/utility/functional/bind). This is semantically closer to an anonymous function in Julia, though it *binds* the arguments in a way similar to `Fix1` and `Fix2` do:

```cpp
void f(int n1, int n2, int n3, const int& n4, int n5);
 
int main() {
    using namespace std::placeholders

    auto f2 = std::bind(f, _3, std::bind(g, _3), _3, 4, 5);
    f2(10, 11, 12)  // f(12, g(12), 12, 4, 5)
}
```

</details>

The C++ approach was also briefly mentioned on discourse [back in 2018](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/fix1-analogue-of-base-fix2/10161/12).

Users implementing `show` methods for an `AbstractString` will have a broken display if the preferred MIME type `istextmime`. A motivating example is in Typstry.jl, which implements `show` for `"image/png"` and `"image/svg+xml"`. Both of these methods work in Pluto.jl, but only `"image/png"` works in IJulia.jl. This is because `"image/svg+xml"` is preferred over `"image/png"`, but this format assumes that an `AbstractString` is ["raw data"](https://github.com/JuliaLang/IJulia.jl/blob/185533665803d5c0f9de6103d0c386422d9e1c6a/src/inline.jl#L24). [Instead of dispatching to `show`, it is returned as a `String`](https://github.com/JuliaLang/IJulia.jl/blob/185533665803d5c0f9de6103d0c386422d9e1c6a/src/inline.jl#L41-L43).

I don't really understand this assumption, why is it being used? The only thing I have found so far is the same assumption in [`repr`](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/io-network/#Base.repr-Tuple{MIME,%20Any}) and presume that's where it came from. A notable difference is that users are able to implement `repr` to patch in a fix.

### Possible Solutions

- Remove the assumption the an `AbstractString` is "raw data"
    - I don't know if or what relies on this assumption
- Export `israwtext`
    - Users can implement this to override the assumption
    - Requires packages to depend on IJulia.jl
- Change [this line](https://github.com/JuliaLang/IJulia.jl/blob/185533665803d5c0f9de6103d0c386422d9e1c6a/src/inline.jl#L41) to `String(repr(mime, x; context = InlineIOContext(buf)))`
    - Users can implement a fix for `repr`, while preserving the preexisting behavior otherwise because `repr` uses the same assumption
    - Extra work to convert some values to a `String`

### 1. The output of `versioninfo()`

IJulia v0.24.2

```
julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.10.3
Commit 0b4590a5507 (2024-04-30 10:59 UTC)
Build Info:
  Official https://julialang.org/ release
Platform Info:
  OS: Linux (x86_64-linux-gnu)
  CPU: 8 × 11th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-1165G7 @ 2.80GHz
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-15.0.7 (ORCJIT, tigerlake)
Threads: 8 default, 0 interactive, 4 GC (on 8 virtual cores)
Environment:
  JULIA_EDITOR = hx
```

### 2. How you installed Julia

juliaup

### 3. A minimal working example (MWE), also known as a minimum reproducible example

```julia
julia> using IJulia

julia> struct S <: AbstractString end

julia> Base.ncodeunits(::S) = 0;

julia> Base.iterate(::S) = nothing;

julia> Base.show(io::IO, ::MIME"image/svg+xml", ::S) = print(io, "s");

julia> S()
""

julia> show(stdout, "image/svg+xml", S())
s
julia> IJulia.display_dict(S())
Dict{String, Union{String, JSON.Writer.JSONText}} with 2 entries:
  "text/plain"    => "\"\""
  "image/svg+xml" => ""
```

```text
julia> ;
shell> echo "Hello World"
Error: nu::parser::shell_andand

  × The '&&' operator is not supported in Nushell
   ╭─[source:1:22]
 1 │ (echo 'Hello World') && true
   ·                      ─┬
   ·                       ╰── instead of '&&', use ';' or 'and'
   ╰────
  help: use ';' instead of the shell '&&', or 'and' instead of the boolean '&&'
```

I think the Julia REPL should either be made compatible with nushell, or it should run commands in a compatible shell like bash. Right now I can't seem to do anything in the shell mode because my default shell is nushell.

Context: Nushell is a new type of cross-platform shell written in pure rust: https://github.com/nushell/nushell/. It would be better to have a single convention for what `func(f, xs)` does.

```jl
julia> maximum(abs, [-1, -2])
2

julia> unique(abs, [-1, -2])
2-element Vector{Int64}:
 -1
 -2

julia> sort([-1, -2]; by=abs)
2-element Vector{Int64}:
 -1
 -2
```

This is more like `sort(xs; by=f)`, which I find clearer about what it is doing than the current design. So proposal:

```jl
unique(xs; by=f)
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50406/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50406/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"reopened"},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1623717244","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50406#issuecomment-1623717244","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50406","id":1623717244,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5gx_V8","user":{"login":"jakobjpeters","id":59785931,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU5Nzg1OTMx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/59785931?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobjpeters","html_url":"https://github.com/jakobjpeters","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobjpeters/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobjpeters/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobjpeters/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobjpeters/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobjpeters/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobjpeters/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobjpeters/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobjpeters/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobjpeters/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-07-06T13:50:09Z","updated_at":"2023-07-06T13:50:09Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"I see what you're going for here, but the existing order has a well established purpose.
